one_line_takeaway: CleanSight shows that fine-tuning-induced LVLM backdoors manifest as cross-modal 'attention stealing' from text to trigger-bearing visual tokens, and that detecting and pruning those high-attention tokens at test time drives ASR to near-zero across six attacks and four datasets while leaving clean-input utility essentially intact -- clearly outperforming pixel-space purification baselines (ST defense, BDMAE, SampDetox, ZIP) that only partially suppress ASR and often degrade clean utility.
key_finding: CleanSight drives ASR to near-zero (0% in most cells, up to only ~5% in the hardest case, TrojVLM/BadNet combinations) across all six attacks and four datasets while leaving clean utility essentially unchanged from the undefended model. Representative Table 1 (VQAv2) numbers: No defense ASR=100.00/CU=62.89 (BadNet) vs CleanSight ASR=0/CU=62.63; Blended No-defense ASR=100.00/CU=67.06 vs CleanSight ASR=0/CU=68.36; TrojVLM is the hardest case, CleanSight ASR=3.14/CU=65.46 vs No-defense ASR=100.00/CU=68.36. On OKVQA, CleanSight ASR ranges 0-4.74% vs 99.22-100% undefended. On COCO/Flickr8k captioning, CleanSight ASR is 0-2.34% (CIDEr near-unchanged) vs 92-100% undefended. The strongest pixel-based baseline (ZIP) only partially reduces ASR (e.g., VQAv2/WaNet ZIP ASR=7.03 but VQAv2/Blended ZIP ASR=84.77; OKVQA/WaNet ZIP ASR=3.91 but most other cells 55-99%), and several pixel-based defenses (ST defense, BDMAE) substantially degrade clean utility (e.g., ST defense vs ISSBA on VQAv2: CU drops to 9.38 from 65.49). Ablation (Table 3, VQAv2, oracle-free): removing the attention-stealing detector (pruning all inputs) keeps ASR at 0 but lowers CU (e.g., 52.47 vs 62.63 for BadNet); removing selective pruning (random 90% token removal) leaves ASR near 100% (100.00, 100.00, 98.83), showing the pruning targeting -- not just token removal per se -- is what neutralizes the backdoor. Generalization: Table 4 shows CleanSight reduces ASR to near-0 across LLaVA-1.5-7B(LoRA), LLaVA-1.5-13B(Adapter), InstructBLIP-7B, and Qwen-VL2-7B, versus ZIP which leaves substantial residual ASR (e.g., 74.61-98.05% on some model/attack combinations) with larger CU losses. Detection-side ablations show TPR stays near 100% across gamma and |Dval| settings, and even with only ~10 clean validation samples FPR can be pushed below ~20%.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is CleanSight, a training-free, plug-and-play, test-time defense against backdoor attacks in fine-tuned large vision-language models (LVLMs). It rests on a mechanistic finding the authors report as their own (not attributed to prior work): backdoor triggers in fine-tuned LVLMs act not through low-level pixel patterns but through 'attention stealing' -- an abnormal cross-modal attention redistribution in which trigger-bearing visual tokens draw attention away from the textual prompt in middle (cross-modal fusion) transformer layers. Building on this, CleanSight (i) detects poisoned inputs by computing a head-specific vision-to-text attention ratio in selected middle layers, aggregating it into a feature vector, and scoring it against a clean reference distribution (mean/std estimated from a small clean validation set) via a whitened L2 deviation score compared to a high-quantile threshold (gamma, default 0.99); and (ii) purifies flagged inputs by unioning, across attention heads, the visual tokens whose attention exceeds a threshold tau into a global mask, then suppressing those tokens in all subsequent layers/decoding steps by adding a large negative bias to their pre-softmax attention logits.
threat_model: Adversary controls the LVLM fine-tuning pipeline (e.g., fine-tuning-as-a-service, where users have no control over training) and can inject poisoned image-query-answer samples with trigger patterns into the fine-tuning set, but is restricted to modifying only the tunable components (adapter/LoRA modules) -- the frozen visual encoder and LLM backbone are assumed untouched because tampering with public backbone weights would be detectable via checksum/weight-diff. The resulting backdoored model behaves normally on clean inputs (x,q) -> o but produces an attacker-specified output o* whenever the trigger Theta is present in the image at inference time. The defender (CleanSight) controls the inference stack and operates purely at test time without retraining or access to the fine-tuning data/process.
limitations: The paper itself notes: (1) TrojVLM is harder to defend against than the other five attacks because its semantic-preserving optimization entangles backdoor behavior with the clean task objective, producing only a mild attention shift that is harder to fully identify/prune (residual ASR up to ~4.74% rather than 0). (2) The logit-lens analysis shows a small late-layer (28th-29th) drop in the realigned trajectory after CleanSight intervention, which the authors say 'calls for further investigation into the residual dynamics of late-layer representations' -- i.e., an acknowledged imperfection even where headline ASR is 0. (3) The method assumes the adversary cannot modify the frozen backbone/visual-encoder weights (only tunable adapter/LoRA parameters), a scope restriction baked into the threat model rather than a claim of full generality against backbone-level attacks. (4) Detection layer selection (ℓs) is tuned per prior analysis (cited to Zhang et al.) with hyperparameters (gamma=0.99, tau=1e-4, 3 detection layers starting at layer 10) fixed for the reported experiments; the ablations explore some sensitivity but the core numbers are for one hyperparameter configuration. (5) No formal adaptive-attacker evaluation appears in the main text (deferred to an appendix on 'adaptive attackers, low poisoning rates, and more models' not captured in the extracted main-body text).

quantitative_results: VQAv2/BadNet: No-defense ASR=100.00 CU=62.89 vs CleanSight ASR=0 CU=62.63
VQAv2/Blended: No-defense ASR=100.00 CU=67.06 vs CleanSight ASR=0 CU=68.36
VQAv2/TrojVLM (hardest case): No-defense ASR=100.00 CU=68.36 vs CleanSight ASR=3.14 CU=65.46
OKVQA/VLOOD: No-defense ASR=100.00 CU=66.54 vs CleanSight ASR=0 CU=53.26
COCO/BadNet: No-defense ASR=99.22 CU=135.70 (CIDEr) vs CleanSight ASR=0 CU=133.28
Flickr8k/BadNet: No-defense ASR=99.61 CU=133.23 vs CleanSight ASR=1.56 CU=131.15
Best pixel-based baseline ZIP: VQAv2/WaNet ASR=7.03 but VQAv2/Blended ASR=84.77 (inconsistent across attacks)
ST defense degrades clean utility sharply on VQAv2/ISSBA: CU drops to 9.38 from undefended 65.49
Ablation (Table 3, VQAv2): removing selective pruning (random 90% token removal) leaves ASR at 100.00/100.00/98.83 for BadNet/Blended/ISSBA respectively, vs 0/0/3.14 for full CleanSight
Cross-model generalization (Table 4, VQAv2): CleanSight vs ZIP on Qwen-VL2-7B BadNet: ASR 0 (CU 73.89) vs ASR 98.05 (CU 69.27)
Detection sensitivity: with as few as ~10 clean validation samples, FPR can be reduced below ~20% while TPR remains near 100%
